u-\partial_
u A_\mu\). It packages the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4} into a single Lorentz-covariant object.
explanation_orig:
The field tensor is the central object in relativistic electromagnetism because it removes the artificial separation between electric and magnetic fields. Different \cref{Inertial frames}{2.1} may split \(F_{\mu
u}\) into electric and magnetic components differently, but the tensor itself transforms cleanly under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}. Its antisymmetry also explains why it has six independent components: three electric and three magnetic. Indices may be raised or lowered using the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}, allowing equations such as \(\partial_\mu F^{\mu
u}=J^
u\) to be written compactly. In this form, the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} is not a pair of unrelated vector fields but one spacetime field.
example_orig: In a chosen inertial frame, the components \(F_{0i}\) correspond to the electric field components, while the purely spatial components \(F_{ij}\) encode the magnetic field. A boost to another frame changes this split, so part of what one observer calls electric may be partly magnetic for another.
definition_new:
The field tensor \(F_{\mu
u}\) is the antisymmetric tensor that represents the electromagnetic field in covariant form. It is constructed from the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1} by \[ F_{\mu
u}=\partial_\mu A_
u-\partial_
u A_\mu. \]
derivation_new:
Starting from the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1}, take the antisymmetric spacetime derivative \[ F_{\mu
u}=\partial_\mu A_
u-\partial_
u A_\mu. \] The antisymmetry gives \(F_{\mu
u}=-F_{
u\mu}\), so in four dimensions there are six independent components. These components are identified, up to the conventional factors of \(c\) fixed by the choice of units, with the three components of the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and the three components of the \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}. Indices are raised and lowered with the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}.
explanation_new: The field tensor is the compact relativistic package for electromagnetism. Electric and magnetic fields can look like separate entities in three-vector notation, but in relativity they are frame-dependent components of one tensor. A change of inertial frame can mix what one observer calls electric with what another calls magnetic. The antisymmetry of \(F_{\mu\nu}\) is also doing work: it encodes the structure that leads to homogeneous Maxwell equations and gauge invariance. The tensor is the object behind the observer-dependent \(\mathbf E\)/\(\mathbf B\) split.

label: Electric field
body_orig: One component of the relativistic \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.   In terms of the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}, the electric field components are  \[ E_i = F_{0i}. \]  The electric field describes the force acting on charges at rest in a given \cref{Inertial frame}{2.1}.   Under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}, electric and magnetic fields mix with one another.
definition_orig: The electric field is the part of the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} that acts on electric charge even when the charge is instantaneously at rest in a chosen \cref{Inertial frame}{2.1}. In relativistic notation its components appear among the time-space components of the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.
explanation_orig: Classically, the electric field is introduced as the force per unit charge. Special relativity keeps that operational meaning but shows that the electric field is not a standalone invariant object. It is one frame-dependent decomposition of the field tensor. When observers change inertial frame, the electric and magnetic components can mix according to the \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}. This is why magnetism can be understood, in part, as a relativistic companion of electricity: what is purely electric in one frame may include a magnetic component in another. The invariant object is the full field tensor, not \(\mathbf E\) by itself.
example_orig: A charge sitting near a charged plate feels an electric force in the plate\'s rest frame. An observer moving parallel to the plate describes the same physical situation with altered charge densities and currents, and in that description magnetic effects may also appear.
definition_new: The electric field is the frame-dependent part of the electromagnetic field that acts on a charge even when the charge is instantaneously at rest in a chosen \cref{Inertial frames}{2.1}. In relativistic notation it is read from the time-space components of the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.
derivation_new: Choose an inertial frame and decompose the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} into time-space and space-space components. The electric field comes from the time-space components, conventionally \(F_{0i}\) or \(F^{0i}\) up to factors and signs determined by the definitions of \(A^\mu\), \(A_\mu\), and \(c\). In three-vector potential language this gives \[ \mathbf E=-\nabla\phi-\frac{\partial\mathbf A}{\partial t}. \]
explanation_new: The electric field is the part of the electromagnetic field that shows up as force on a charge at rest in a chosen inertial frame. That phrase 'in a chosen frame' is important. Another observer moving relative to the charge may split the same electromagnetic field differently into electric and magnetic parts. In everyday examples, a positive source charge has outward electric field lines and a positive test charge feels a force along them. Relativistically, those familiar arrows are one frame's view of components inside the field tensor.

label: Magnetic field
body_orig: The second component of the relativistic \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.   Its components arise from the spatial parts of the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.    Magnetic fields act on moving charges and currents.   In special relativity the magnetic field is not an independent entity but rather part of the same geometric structure as the \cref{Electric field}{7.3}.
definition_orig: The magnetic field is the part of the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} associated with forces on moving charges and currents in a chosen inertial frame. In the relativistic formulation it is encoded in the spatial components of the antisymmetric \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.
explanation_orig: The magnetic field is not an independent substance added to the electric field. It is another frame-dependent aspect of the same electromagnetic field tensor. In one frame a charge distribution may be described mainly by an electric field; in another, where the charges are moving, currents appear and a magnetic field is present. This unification explains why \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and magnetic field transform into one another under changes of inertial frame. Magnetic fields are therefore most naturally understood as part of a single relativistic object whose dynamics are governed by \cref{Maxwell\'s equations}{7.6}.
example_orig: A steady current in a wire produces a magnetic field circling the wire. A moving test charge near the wire feels a magnetic part of the Lorentz force, while another observer may describe the same interaction with a different mixture of electric and magnetic fields.
definition_new: The magnetic field is the frame-dependent part of the electromagnetic field associated with forces on moving charges and currents. In covariant notation it is read from the spatial components of the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.
derivation_new: After choosing an inertial frame, decompose the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}. The purely spatial antisymmetric components \(F_{ij}\) encode the magnetic field, again with signs fixed by the convention for \(F_{\mu\nu}\). In terms of the vector potential, the corresponding three-vector relation is \[ \mathbf B=\nabla\times\mathbf A. \]
explanation_new: The magnetic field is easiest to remember as the part of the electromagnetic field tied to moving charges and currents, and to sideways forces on moving test charges. But relativity adds a deeper lesson: magnetic and electric fields are not independent substances. They are different frame-dependent aspects of the same field tensor. A current-carrying wire may be described with magnetic effects in one frame and with a different balance of electric and magnetic effects in another. The circular field around a current is the familiar picture; the tensor is the covariant one.

label: Electromagnetic field
body_orig: The unified relativistic field combining the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}.   This field is fully represented by the antisymmetric \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.    The dynamics of the electromagnetic field are governed by \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}, which follow from an electromagnetic \cref{Field Lagrangian}{6.3}.
definition_orig: The electromagnetic field is the unified relativistic field whose frame-dependent components are the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}. It is represented compactly by the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}, which describes how charges and currents interact across spacetime.
explanation_orig: Before relativity it is tempting to regard electric and magnetic fields as separate physical entities. Special relativity shows that they are two aspects of a single field. Which components are called electric and which are called magnetic depends on the observer\'s inertial frame, but the underlying tensorial field is the same. This is a powerful example of a broader theme: physical theories should be expressed using objects that transform consistently between observers. The electromagnetic field has its own dynamics, described by \cref{Maxwell\'s equations}{7.6}, and those dynamics can be derived from an electromagnetic \cref{Field Lagrangian}{6.3}. It can carry energy, momentum, and stresses, developed later through the energy-momentum description of fields.
example_orig: An electromagnetic wave travelling through vacuum is not an electric wave plus a separate magnetic wave. It is a single propagating electromagnetic disturbance whose electric and magnetic components are different projections of the same underlying field.
definition_new: The electromagnetic field is the unified relativistic field whose frame-dependent components are the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}. Covariantly, it is represented by the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.
derivation_new: The unified electromagnetic field is obtained conceptually by collecting the electric and magnetic components into the antisymmetric tensor \(F_{\mu\nu}\). In a chosen frame, \cref{Electric field}{7.3} components occupy the time-space part of the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}, while \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4} components occupy the spatial antisymmetric part. Under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}, those components mix, so the covariant object is the tensor, not the separate three-vectors.
explanation_new: The electromagnetic field is the unified physical field whose frame-dependent faces are \(\mathbf E\) and \(\mathbf B\). This is more than a vocabulary upgrade. It explains why electric and magnetic effects transform into each other between observers, why the field can carry energy and momentum, and why a local field description replaces instantaneous action at a distance. A useful mental model is that \(F_{\mu\nu}\) is the underlying object, while \(\mathbf E\) and \(\mathbf B\) are what a particular observer reads off from it.

label: Maxwell's equations
body_orig: The relativistic field equations governing the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.   In compact covariant form they may be written  \[ \partial_\mu F^{\mu\nu} = J^\nu. \]  These equations describe how electric charges and currents act as sources for the electromagnetic field.   They arise from the \cref{Euler–Lagrange equations}{5.3} applied to the electromagnetic \cref{Field Lagrangian}{6.3}.
definition_orig: Maxwell\'s equations are the field equations governing the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}. In relativistic notation their sourced part can be written \(\partial_\mu F^{\mu\nu}=J^\nu\), relating the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} to the charge-current source represented by the \cref{Four-current}{8.2}.
explanation_orig: Maxwell\'s equations say how electric charges and currents create electromagnetic fields and how changing fields propagate. Their relativistic form reveals a compact structure that is partly hidden in the usual three-vector equations. The field tensor contains both electric and magnetic fields, while the four-current contains charge density and current density. The equations are local \cref{Field equations}{6.4}: the behaviour of the field at a spacetime event is related to field derivatives and sources at that event. They can be obtained from the \cref{Euler–Lagrange equations}{5.3} applied to the electromagnetic \cref{Field Lagrangian}{6.3}, making electromagnetism an example of the general variational machinery used for relativistic fields.
example_orig: In empty space, where \(J^\nu=0\), Maxwell\'s equations imply that disturbances of the electromagnetic field propagate as waves at speed \(c\). With sources present, the same equations describe fields produced by charges, currents, and radiation from accelerated charges.
definition_new: Maxwell's equations are the relativistic field equations governing the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}. In covariant form they relate the antisymmetric \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} to the source \cref{Four-current}{8.2}, while the homogeneous equations express the fact that the field tensor is built from a potential.
derivation_new:
Maxwell's equations follow by applying the \cref{Euler-Lagrange equations}{5.3} to the electromagnetic \cref{Field Lagrangian}{6.3}. With \(F_{\mu
u}=\partial_\mu A_
u-\partial_
u A_\mu\), a standard electromagnetic Lagrangian density is \[ \mathcal L=- rac{1}{4\mu_0}F_{\mu
u}F^{\mu
u}-j_\mu A^\mu, \] using the \(+---\) metric convention. Varying the action with respect to the potential \(A_\mu\) gives the sourced Maxwell equation \[ \partial_\mu F^{\mu
u}=\mu_0 j^
u. \] The second half of Maxwell's equations follows from the definition of \(F_{\mu
u}\) itself: \[ \partial_\lambda F_{\mu
u}+\partial_\mu F_{
u\lambda}+\partial_
u F_{\lambda\mu}=0. \] Together these equations are local \cref{Field equations}{6.4}: at each spacetime event they relate field derivatives to the charge and current source at that event.
explanation_new: Maxwell's equations are not just four formulas to memorize; they are the local rules governing the electromagnetic field. They connect sources to fields and also allow fields to propagate as waves. In covariant notation, much of the structure becomes shorter: sources enter through the four-current, and the antisymmetric field tensor carries the electric and magnetic components together. If the sources vanish in a region, the equations still have nontrivial wave solutions. That is how light appears as a self-propagating electromagnetic disturbance rather than as a separate kind of object.

label: Lorenz gauge
body_orig: A condition imposed on the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1}:  \[ \partial_\mu A^\mu = 0. \]  This gauge choice simplifies \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}, allowing them to be written as wave equations for the potential.   The physical \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} remains unchanged under such gauge transformations.
definition_orig: The Lorenz gauge is the condition \(\partial_\mu A^\mu=0\) imposed on the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1}. It is a particular gauge choice that simplifies the potential form of \cref{Maxwell\'s equations}{7.6} while leaving the physical \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} unchanged.
explanation_orig: Because the vector potential is not unique, one may impose an additional condition to choose a convenient representative from a family of physically equivalent potentials. The Lorenz gauge is especially useful in relativistic electromagnetism because it is itself Lorentz-covariant and turns the equations for the potential into a clean wave-equation form. It does not add a new physical law; it is a calculational and representational choice allowed by \cref{Gauge invariance}{8.1}. The name is often confused with Lorentz, but the gauge is named after Ludvig Lorenz. Later, the broader idea of selecting such a representative is described as \cref{Gauge fixing}{11.2}.
example_orig: After imposing the Lorenz gauge, Maxwell\'s equations for the four-potential take a form schematically like a wave equation with source: \(\Box A^\mu = J^\mu\), up to conventional constants and sign choices. This makes the propagation of electromagnetic influences especially transparent.
definition_new: The Lorenz gauge is the covariant gauge condition \[ \partial_\mu A^\mu=0 \] imposed on the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1}. It selects a convenient representative from a gauge-equivalent family of potentials without changing the physical \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.
derivation_new: Because of \cref{Gauge invariance}{8.1}, potentials related by \(A_\mu\rightarrow A_\mu+\partial_\mu\Lambda\) describe the same field tensor. The \cref{Vector potential}{7.1} therefore contains redundant descriptive freedom. The Lorenz gauge fixes part of that freedom by imposing the Lorentz-covariant scalar condition \[ \partial_\mu A^\mu=0. \] With this condition, the potential form of \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6} simplifies to a wave equation with source, schematically \(\Box A^\mu=\mu_0 j^\mu\) in SI conventions.
explanation_new: The Lorenz gauge is a choice that makes the potential description cleaner without changing the physical field. Gauge conditions can feel suspicious at first: are we changing the physics? No. We are choosing one representative from many equivalent potentials, much like choosing convenient coordinates. The Lorenz gauge condition \(\partial_\mu A^\mu=0\) is especially useful because it respects Lorentz covariance and turns Maxwell's equations for the potential into a wave-equation form. Remember the spelling: Lorenz gauge, not Lorentz transformation.

label: Gauge invariance
body_orig: A fundamental symmetry of the electromagnetic theory in which the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1} can be transformed according to  \[ A_\mu \rightarrow A_\mu + \partial_\mu \Lambda \]  without changing the physical \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}. Because the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} depends only on derivatives of the potential, it remains unchanged under such transformations.  Gauge invariance therefore reflects a redundancy in the description of the electromagnetic field.  The freedom to choose different potentials describing the same physics leads naturally to conditions such as the \cref{Lorenz gauge}{7.7}.
definition_orig: Gauge invariance is the freedom to change the electromagnetic \cref{Vector potential}{7.1} without changing the physical \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}. In electromagnetism the transformation \(A_\mu \rightarrow A_\mu + \partial_\mu \Lambda\) leaves the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} unchanged, so different potentials can describe the same physical field.
explanation_orig: Gauge invariance is a symmetry of description rather than a symmetry that moves a physical object. The vector potential contains more mathematical information than is directly observable, and some of that information is redundant. What matters physically is the field tensor, which is built from derivatives of the potential in such a way that the added gradient cancels out. This redundancy is not a nuisance: it is a powerful organizing principle. It constrains the form of the electromagnetic theory, explains why conditions such as the \cref{Lorenz gauge}{7.7} are choices rather than new physics, and connects naturally with the symmetry ideas behind \cref{Noether's theorem}{5.6}. In later field theory, gauge invariance becomes one of the central principles used to build interactions.
example_orig: Two potentials that differ by \(\partial_\mu\Lambda\) may assign different values to \(A_\mu\) at each event, but they produce the same electric and magnetic fields. Choosing the Lorenz gauge is like choosing a convenient coordinate grid on this space of equivalent descriptions; it simplifies calculations without changing measurable predictions.
definition_new: Gauge invariance is the freedom to change the electromagnetic potential by \[ A_\mu\rightarrow A_\mu+\partial_\mu\Lambda \] without changing the physical electromagnetic field. The \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} remains invariant under this transformation.
derivation_new:
The invariance follows from the construction of the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}. Under \(A_\muightarrow A_\mu+\partial_\mu\Lambda\), \[ F_{\mu
u}ightarrow \partial_\mu(A_
u+\partial_
u\Lambda)-\partial_
u(A_\mu+\partial_\mu\Lambda). \] The added terms cancel because partial derivatives commute: \[ \partial_\mu\partial_
u\Lambda-\partial_
u\partial_\mu\Lambda=0. \] Therefore \(F_{\mu
u}\), and hence the physical \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}, is unchanged.
explanation_new: Gauge invariance says the potential has redundancy: different \(A_\mu\) fields can produce the same \(F_{\mu\nu}\). This can be hard to accept because we are used to different mathematical descriptions meaning different physical situations. Here the field tensor is the observable electromagnetic object, and the potential has extra descriptive freedom. An analogy is choosing coordinates on a map: changing coordinates changes the labels, not the terrain. Gauge invariance is deeper than that analogy, but it helps with the first conceptual step: not every change in the potential is a physical change.

label: Four-current
body_orig: The relativistic quantity describing the distribution and motion of electric charge.  It forms a \cref{Four-vector}{4.2} with components  \[ J^\mu = (c\rho,\vec J), \]  where \( \rho \) is charge density and \( \vec J \) is current density.  The four-current acts as the source term in \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}. Its conservation is expressed through the continuity equation discussed under \cref{Charge conservation}{8.5}.
definition_orig: The four-current is the relativistic object that combines electric charge density and ordinary current density into a single \cref{Four-vector}{4.2}. It is usually written \(J^\mu=(c\rho,\vec J)\), where \(\rho\) is charge density and \(\vec J\) is the three-current density.
explanation_orig: In non-relativistic language charge density and current density look like separate quantities, but different inertial observers mix space and time when they describe the same physical situation. A distribution that one observer calls mostly charge density may be described by another as charge plus current. The four-current packages these descriptions into one covariant quantity. It is the source term in the covariant form of \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}, just as mass density is the source in Newtonian gravity. Because it is a local density-and-flux object, it fits naturally with the \cref{Principle of locality}{2.3}: electromagnetic fields at an event respond to the charge and current structure represented there.
example_orig: A line of charges at rest has charge density but no current in its own rest frame. An observer moving along the line sees length contraction and moving charge, so both \(\rho\) and \(\vec J\) change. The four-current is the covariant object that relates these two descriptions.
definition_new: The four-current \(j^\mu\) combines charge density and current density into one relativistic \cref{Four-vectors}{4.2}: \[ j^\mu=(c\rho,\mathbf j). \] It is the source appearing in covariant \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}.
derivation_new: The four-current is built by packaging local charge density and ordinary current density into a four-vector. Since charge density is the time component of charge flow and \(\mathbf j\) is the spatial flux of charge, the covariant object is \[ j^\mu=(c\rho,j_x,j_y,j_z). \] Its transformation law is the \cref{Four-vectors}{4.2} transformation law. Local conservation is expressed by its four-divergence, \(\partial_\mu j^\mu=0\), developed in \cref{Charge conservation}{8.5}.
explanation_new: The four-current packages charge density and current density into one relativistic source object. Charge density alone is not enough because different observers disagree about densities and currents when charges are moving. What one observer calls mostly charge density, another may partly describe as current. The four-current keeps the bookkeeping together, just as four-momentum keeps energy and momentum together. In a wire, the lab frame and a frame moving with charges can assign different \(\rho\) and \(\mathbf j\), but they are related as components of \(j^\mu\).

label: Minimal coupling
body_orig: The prescription for introducing electromagnetic interaction into the \cref{Lagrangian}{5.2} of a charged particle.  The ordinary momentum is replaced by  \[ p_\mu \rightarrow p_\mu - eA_\mu, \]  where \(A_\mu\) is the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1}.   This modification preserves \cref{Gauge invariance}{8.1} and leads directly to the dynamical law governing charged particles in electromagnetic fields.
definition_orig: Minimal coupling is the standard prescription for introducing electromagnetic interaction into the dynamics of a charged particle. In relativistic notation it replaces the free-particle momentum structure by the gauge-covariant combination \(p_\mu-eA_\mu\), where \(A_\mu\) is the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1}.
explanation_orig: The phrase “minimal” means that the interaction is introduced in the simplest way compatible with relativity and \cref{Gauge invariance}{8.1}. Instead of adding an arbitrary new force law by hand, one modifies the particle \cref{Lagrangian}{5.2} or momentum in a way that makes the electromagnetic potential part of the dynamical structure. This also clarifies the distinction between mechanical momentum and \cref{Canonical momentum}{5.5}: in the presence of a vector potential, the canonical momentum includes a contribution from the field. Applying the \cref{Action principle}{5.1} to the minimally coupled particle then gives the \cref{Lorentz force law}{8.4}.
example_orig: For a charged particle, the free-particle action is supplemented by an interaction term involving \(A_\mu dx^\mu\). Varying this action does not merely produce straight-line inertial motion; it produces acceleration determined by the electromagnetic field tensor.
definition_new: Minimal coupling is the standard prescription for introducing electromagnetic interaction into a charged particle's dynamics by replacing ordinary momentum structure with the gauge-covariant combination involving the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1}, commonly \(p_\mu-eA_\mu\).
derivation_new: The prescription is guided by \cref{Gauge invariance}{8.1} and the existence of the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1}. For a charged particle, one adds the simplest local interaction term between the particle worldline and the potential, proportional to \[ e A_\mu dx^\mu. \] Equivalently, in momentum language the free momentum is replaced by the gauge-covariant combination \[ p_\mu\rightarrow p_\mu-eA_\mu. \] Varying the minimally coupled action gives the \cref{Lorentz force law}{8.4}.
explanation_new: Minimal coupling is the economical way to make a charged particle interact with electromagnetism. Instead of inventing a separate force rule first, we insert the vector potential into the particle's action or momentum structure. The phrase 'minimal' means no unnecessary extra terms: use the simplest local, gauge-compatible coupling. This also explains why canonical momentum and mechanical momentum can differ. In the nonrelativistic limit, the Lagrangian picks up terms involving \(\phi\) and \(\mathbf A\), and varying it gives the Lorentz force.

label: Lorentz force law
body_orig:
The relativistic equation of motion for a charged particle interacting with the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.  In covariant form it can be written  \[  rac{dp^\mu}{d	au} = eF^{\mu
u}u_
u, \]  where \(p^\mu\) is the \cref{Momentum four-vector}{4.5}, \(u^\mu\) is the \cref{Velocity four-vector}{4.4}, and \(F^{\mu
u}\) is the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.  This equation arises naturally from applying the \cref{Action principle}{5.1} to a charged particle interacting with the electromagnetic field via \cref{Minimal coupling}{8.3}.
definition_orig: The Lorentz force law is the equation of motion for a charged particle in an electromagnetic field. In covariant form it may be written \(dp^\mu/d\tau=eF^{\mu\nu}u_\nu\), relating the change of \cref{Momentum four-vector}{4.5} to the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} and the particle's \cref{Velocity four-vector}{4.4}.
explanation_orig: This law says how the electromagnetic field acts on matter. The covariant expression is compact because it treats electric and magnetic effects as different components of the same spacetime object. When unpacked into ordinary three-vector language, it contains the familiar electric force on a charge and the velocity-dependent magnetic force. Its relativistic form also shows that the force is not an independent add-on to electromagnetism: it follows from \cref{Minimal coupling}{8.3} and the \cref{Action principle}{5.1}. The law therefore links particle dynamics to field theory, connecting the motion of charged particles with the same \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} governed by \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}.
example_orig: A charge initially at rest in a uniform electric field accelerates along the field direction. A charge already moving through a magnetic field can be deflected sideways, producing circular or helical motion. Both effects are contained in the single covariant equation using \(F^{\mu\nu}\).
definition_new: The Lorentz force law is the relativistic equation of motion for a charged particle in an electromagnetic field. In covariant form it relates the rate of change of \cref{Momentum four-vector}{4.5} to the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} and the particle's \cref{Velocity four-vector}{4.4}.
explanation_new: The Lorentz force law is the bridge from field to particle motion. In three-vector form it says electric fields push charges along the field and magnetic fields deflect moving charges sideways. In covariant form the same statement becomes one compact equation involving \(F^\mu{}_{\nu}\), four-velocity, and four-momentum. The compact notation is not just elegance; it shows that electric and magnetic forces are components of one relativistic interaction. A charge at rest in an electric field and a moving charge curving in a magnetic field are two familiar faces of the same law.

label: Charge conservation
body_orig: Electric charge is locally conserved.  In relativistic form this statement is expressed by the continuity equation  \[ \partial_\mu J^\mu = 0, \]  where \(J^\mu\) is the \cref{Four-current}{8.2}.   This relation ensures the internal consistency of \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6} and reflects the symmetry structure of the electromagnetic theory.
definition_orig: Charge conservation is the statement that electric charge cannot be locally created or destroyed. In relativistic notation it is expressed by the continuity equation \(\partial_\mu J^\mu=0\), where \(J^\mu\) is the \cref{Four-current}{8.2}.
explanation_orig: The word “locally” is important. Charge conservation does not merely say that the total charge of the universe is constant; it says that charge can leave a region only by flowing through its boundary. The continuity equation expresses this bookkeeping at each spacetime event. It is also required for consistency of the sourced form of \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}: the field equations cannot be compatible with a source whose charge simply appears or disappears. More conceptually, charge conservation is closely tied to the symmetry structure of electromagnetism, especially \cref{Gauge invariance}{8.1} and the conservation-law ideas summarized by \cref{Noether's theorem}{5.6}.
example_orig: If the charge density inside a small box decreases, the continuity equation says there must be a corresponding outward current through the surface of the box. The lost charge has gone somewhere; it has not vanished at that event.
definition_new: Charge conservation is the local statement that electric charge cannot appear or disappear. In relativistic notation it is written as the continuity equation for the \cref{Four-current}{8.2}: \[ \partial_\mu j^\mu=0. \]
derivation_new:
Charge conservation follows from the sourced \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}. Taking the divergence of \[ \partial_\mu F^{\mu
u}=\mu_0 j^
u \] gives \[ \partial_
u\partial_\mu F^{\mu
u}=\mu_0\partial_
u j^
u. \] The left-hand side vanishes because \(F^{\mu
u}\) is antisymmetric while the double derivative is symmetric in \(\mu\) and \(
u\). Therefore \[ \partial_\mu j^\mu=0. \] This is the covariant continuity equation for the \cref{Four-current}{8.2}.
explanation_new: Charge conservation is local bookkeeping. It does not merely say the total charge of the universe stays fixed; it says charge cannot disappear from a small box unless current flows through the boundary. The continuity equation expresses that point-by-point accounting. In Maxwell theory it also follows from the antisymmetry of the field tensor, so it is built into the consistency of the equations. If charge density in a small region decreases, the equation demands an outward current. The charge went somewhere; it did not vanish at an event.

label: Energy density of EM field
body_orig: The electromagnetic field itself carries energy.  The energy density stored in the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} is  \[ u = \frac12 (E^2 + B^2), \]  where \(E\) and \(B\) denote the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}.   This energy contributes to the relativistic conservation laws described by the \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4}.
definition_new: The electromagnetic energy density is the energy per unit volume carried by the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}. In SI units it is \[ u=\frac12\left(\epsilon_0 E^2+\frac{1}{\mu_0}B^2\right), \] where \(\mathbf E\) and \(\mathbf B\) are the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}.
derivation_new: The energy density is obtained as the time-time component of the electromagnetic \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4}. In a chosen inertial frame, decomposing the field tensor into the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4} gives \[ T^{00}=u=\frac12\left(\epsilon_0 E^2+\frac{1}{\mu_0}B^2\right). \] Thus the energy stored locally in the field is built from the squared electric and magnetic field strengths.
explanation_new: Electromagnetic fields store energy locally. That is a major conceptual step if you first met fields only as devices for exerting forces on charges. The energy is not just in the particles; it can be in the field between them and can move through space. The formula \(u=\tfrac12(\epsilon_0E^2+B^2/\mu_0)\) says stronger fields carry more energy density. In a plane wave, the electric and magnetic contributions are equal, so the field energy is shared between the two components. This energy density is one component of the larger energy-momentum tensor.

label: Momentum density (Poynting vector)
body_orig: Electromagnetic fields also carry momentum.   The momentum density is given by the Poynting vector  \[ \vec S = \vec E \times \vec B. \]  This vector represents the flow of energy and momentum carried by the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.   Together with the \cref{Energy density of EM field}{9.1}, it forms part of the relativistic description of field energy and momentum.
definition_new: The Poynting vector \(\mathbf S\) describes electromagnetic energy flux: the rate at which field energy flows through unit area. In SI units, \[ \mathbf S=\frac{1}{\mu_0}\mathbf E\times\mathbf B. \] It is closely related to the momentum density of the electromagnetic field.
derivation_new: The Poynting vector is obtained from the mixed time-space components of the electromagnetic \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4}. When those components are decomposed into the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}, the energy-flow result from \cref{Field energy}{7.5} gives \[ \mathbf S=\frac{1}{\mu_0}\mathbf E\times\mathbf B. \] The corresponding field momentum density is \[ \mathbf g=\frac{\mathbf S}{c^2}. \]
explanation_new: The Poynting vector tells you where electromagnetic energy is going. Energy density says how much field energy is stored in a region; \(\mathbf S\) says how that energy flows through space. The cross product \(\mathbf E\times\mathbf B\) is a direction cue as well as a formula: in a plane wave with \(\mathbf E\) along \(y\) and \(\mathbf B\) along \(z\), \(\mathbf S\) points along the direction of propagation. Relativity ties this energy flux to momentum density, so field energy flow also means field momentum.

label: Stress–energy of EM field
body_orig: The electromagnetic field exerts stresses and transports energy and momentum through spacetime.   These properties are summarized by the electromagnetic stress–energy tensor, constructed from the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} and the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}.  This tensor describes how electromagnetic energy and momentum propagate and interact with matter.
definition_new: The electromagnetic stress-energy tensor is the electromagnetic-field instance of the general \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4}. It combines electromagnetic energy density, momentum density, energy flux, and stresses into one Lorentz-covariant tensor.
derivation_new:
For the electromagnetic field, the stress-energy tensor is constructed algebraically from the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} and the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}. With the \(+---\) convention and suppressing unit-dependent constants, a common form is \[ T^{\mu
u}=-F^{\mu\lambda}F^
u{}_{\lambda}+ rac14\eta^{\mu
u}F^{\alpha eta}F_{\alpha eta}. \] This is the electromagnetic specialization of the general \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4}.
explanation_new: The stress-energy tensor may sound abstract, but it is a complete local accounting sheet for electromagnetic energy and momentum. The time-time part is energy density, the mixed parts describe energy flux and momentum density, and the spatial parts describe stresses: momentum flowing across surfaces. Radiation pressure is a concrete example. Light hitting a surface carries momentum, so it can push. Different observers split the tensor into density, flux, and stress differently, but they are decomposing the same covariant object.

label: Energy--momentum tensor
body_orig: A rank-two tensor \(T^{\mu\nu}\) describing the density and flow of energy and momentum in spacetime.  For the electromagnetic field it is constructed from the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.  Conservation of energy and momentum is expressed by  \[ \partial_\mu T^{\mu\nu} = 0. \]  This conservation law follows from spacetime translation symmetry via \cref{Noether's theorem}{5.6}.
definition_new: The energy-momentum tensor \(T^{\mu\nu}\) is a rank-two tensor that describes the density and flow of energy and momentum through spacetime. Its conservation equation, \[ \partial_\mu T^{\mu\nu}=0, \] expresses local conservation of energy and momentum for an isolated system.
derivation_new:
The energy-momentum tensor is derived from spacetime translation symmetry using \cref{Noether's theorem}{5.6}. If the action is unchanged under constant shifts \[ x^\mu ightarrow x^\mu+\epsilon^\mu, \] then there is a conserved current for each spacetime direction. These four conserved currents are assembled into \(T^{\mu
u}\), with the conservation law \[ \partial_\mu T^{\mu
u}=0. \] For fields described by a Lagrangian density \(\mathcal L(\phi_a,\partial_\mu\phi_a)\), the canonical form is \[ T^\mu{}_{
u}= rac{\partial\mathcal L}{\partial(\partial_\mu\phi_a)}\partial_
u\phi_a-\delta^\mu{}_{
u}\mathcal L. \] This form may be improved or symmetrized when needed, especially for gauge fields. For electromagnetism, the physically useful symmetric tensor is constructed from the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}. With the \(+---\) metric convention and suppressing unit-dependent constants, \[ T^{\mu
u}=-F^{\mu\lambda}F^
u{}_{\lambda}+ rac14 \eta^{\mu
u}F^{\alpha eta}F_{\alpha eta}. \] This tensor packages electromagnetic energy density, momentum density, and stresses into one Lorentz-covariant object.
explanation_new: The energy-momentum tensor generalizes the idea of energy and momentum conservation to fields and continuous systems. A field can have energy spread through space, momentum flowing from one region to another, and stresses across surfaces. All of that local bookkeeping is held in \(T^{\mu\nu}\). The conservation equation \(\partial_\mu T^{\mu\nu}=0\) says energy-momentum is not disappearing at a point; it is balanced by flow. For electromagnetism, \(T^{00}\) gives field energy density, while other components encode Poynting flux, momentum density, and pressure-like stresses.

label: Electromagnetic waves
body_orig: Self-propagating oscillations of the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}.   In regions where the \cref{Four-current}{8.2} vanishes, the field equations for the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} reduce to wave equations, allowing disturbances in the field to propagate through space.  These waves travel at the speed \(c\), consistent with the \cref{Constancy of the speed of light}{1.2}.   Electromagnetic waves carry energy and momentum, described by the \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4} and transported through space according to the \cref{Momentum density (Poynting vector)}{9.2}.
definition_new: Electromagnetic waves are propagating source-free disturbances of the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}. In a chosen frame they appear as coupled oscillations of the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}, travelling at speed \(c\) in vacuum.
derivation_new: Electromagnetic waves follow from source-free \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}. In a region where the \cref{Four-current}{8.2} vanishes, Maxwell's equations imply a \cref{Wave equation}{10.2} for the field or for the potential in a suitable gauge: \[ \Box A^\mu=0. \] Plane-wave solutions have electric and magnetic components transverse to the propagation direction, and the speed of propagation is fixed by the invariant light speed \(c\).
explanation_new: An electromagnetic wave is a self-sustaining disturbance of one electromagnetic field, not two independent waves called electric and magnetic. Maxwell's equations link the changing electric and magnetic components so the disturbance propagates at \(c\) in vacuum. In a plane wave, \(\mathbf E\), \(\mathbf B\), and the direction of travel are mutually perpendicular, and the Poynting vector points along the propagation direction. The wave carries energy and momentum, which is why light can heat a surface or exert radiation pressure.

label: Wave equation
body_orig: In the \cref{Lorenz gauge}{7.7}, the equations governing the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1} take the form  \[ \square A_\mu = J_\mu, \]  where \(J_\mu\) is the \cref{Four-current}{8.2} and \(\square = \partial_\mu \partial^\mu\) is the d'Alembertian operator.  When the source term vanishes, the potential satisfies a free wave equation. Solutions correspond to \cref{Electromagnetic waves}{10.1} propagating at the speed determined by the spacetime structure encoded in the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}.
definition_new: The relativistic wave equation is a field equation involving the d'Alembertian operator \[ \Box=\partial_\mu\partial^\mu=\frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial t^2}-\nabla^2. \] For electromagnetism, it describes propagating solutions of the vector potential or fields.
derivation_new: The electromagnetic wave equation is obtained from \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}. Writing the field in terms of the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1} and imposing the \cref{Lorenz gauge}{7.7}, \(\partial_\mu A^\mu=0\), the sourced equation becomes schematically \[ \Box A^\mu=\mu_0 j^\mu. \] The operator \(\Box\) is formed using the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}. In source-free regions where the \cref{Four-current}{8.2} vanishes, this reduces to \[ \Box A^\mu=0, \] whose solutions describe \cref{Electromagnetic waves}{10.1}.
explanation_new: The wave equation is the mathematical pattern for finite-speed propagation. With the \(+---\) metric, the d'Alembertian has one time part and three spatial parts: \(\Box=c^{-2}\partial_t^2-\nabla^2\). That sign structure is what makes light-cone propagation appear. In electromagnetism, choosing the Lorenz gauge turns the potential equations into a clean wave equation. In a source-free region, plane waves satisfy \(\omega=ck\), which is the compact statement that the disturbance moves at speed \(c\).

label: Radiation reaction
body_orig: When charged particles accelerate, they emit \cref{Electromagnetic waves}{10.1}.   Because these waves carry energy and momentum described by the \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4}, the emitting particle must experience a recoil force.  This phenomenon modifies the motion predicted by the \cref{Lorentz force law}{8.4} and represents the back–reaction of the field on its source.
definition_new: Radiation reaction is the back-reaction force on an accelerating charged particle caused by the energy and momentum it radiates away as electromagnetic waves.
derivation_new: An accelerating charge emits \cref{Electromagnetic waves}{10.1}. Those waves carry energy and momentum, described locally by the \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4}. Conservation of total energy and momentum then requires the charged particle's motion to be modified: the particle loses energy-momentum to the field it radiates. Radiation reaction is the correction to the motion beyond the applied \cref{Lorentz force law}{8.4}.
explanation_new: Radiation reaction is difficult because the charge is affected by the field it creates. The ordinary Lorentz force describes how an external field acts on a charge. But an accelerating charge emits electromagnetic waves, and those waves carry energy and momentum away. Conservation then says the particle's motion must reflect that loss. A driven electron, for example, radiates while it oscillates, so some work done on it leaves as radiation rather than remaining as particle energy. The details are subtle because self-interaction can lead to unphysical runaway or pre-accelerating solutions if handled carelessly.

label: Lorentz invariance
body_orig: A fundamental symmetry stating that the laws of physics have the same form in all \cref{Inertial frames}{2.1}.   Mathematically, this means the equations governing physical quantities are covariant under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}.  Objects such as \cref{Four-vectors}{4.2}, the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}, and the \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4} transform in ways that preserve the invariant structure defined by the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}.   Lorentz invariance is therefore a guiding principle in constructing relativistic physical theories.
definition_new: Lorentz invariance is the statement that the laws of physics have the same form in all inertial frames related by \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}. Equivalently, physical equations should be expressible using Lorentz-covariant objects and invariant contractions.
derivation_new: Lorentz invariance is the modern mathematical expression of the \cref{Principle of relativity}{1.1} in Minkowski spacetime. The relevant transformations are the \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}, defined by preserving the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}: \[ \Lambda^T\eta\Lambda=\eta. \] Objects such as \cref{Four-vectors}{4.2}, the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}, and the \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4} transform in ways that preserve tensor equations between inertial frames.
explanation_new: Lorentz invariance is the demand that the form of physical law does not depend on which inertial observer writes it down. This does not mean every observer measures the same components. They usually do not. It means the equations relate objects that transform consistently, so all observers describe the same physics. Tensor notation is helpful because it makes this structure visible: if both sides of an equation are tensors of the same type, covariance is built in. For example, \(p_\mu p^\mu=m^2c^2\) is invariant, while Maxwell's equations in tensor form are covariant.

label: Gauge fixing
body_orig: Because of \cref{Gauge invariance}{8.1}, many different \cref{Vector potentials}{7.1} correspond to the same physical \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.   Gauge fixing is the procedure of choosing a particular representative from this equivalence class.  An example is the \cref{Lorenz gauge}{7.7}, which simplifies the equations governing the potential by turning \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6} into wave equations.   Although different gauge choices alter the mathematical form of the potential, observable quantities derived from the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} remain unchanged.
definition_new: Gauge fixing is the choice of one representative potential from a family of gauge-equivalent potentials. It removes redundant descriptive freedom associated with \cref{Gauge invariance}{8.1} without changing the physical electromagnetic field.
derivation_new: Gauge fixing is possible because \cref{Gauge invariance}{8.1} says that many potentials describe the same physical field. The \cref{Vector potential}{7.1} may be changed while leaving the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} and hence the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} unchanged. A gauge condition adds an extra equation selecting a convenient representative. The \cref{Lorenz gauge}{7.7}, \(\partial_\mu A^\mu=0\), is one such choice and is useful because it respects \cref{Lorentz invariance}{11.1}.
explanation_new: Gauge fixing is choosing a convenient representative from many physically equivalent potentials. The danger is to think the gauge choice is an extra physical assumption. It is not; it is a way to remove redundant description. The field tensor remains the same. This is similar in spirit to choosing coordinates, though gauge symmetry has its own physics. The Lorenz gauge is a useful example because it preserves Lorentz covariance and simplifies Maxwell's equations for the potential to a wave-equation form, \(\Box A^\mu=\mu_0j^\mu\).
